title: Fall 2026 Fulcrum Journalism Fellowship Opens Applications for U.S. Journalism Students with $1,000 Stipend
summary: Applications are now open for the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship, a paid journalism training and mentorship opportunity created for undergraduate and graduate journalism students studying in the United States. The fellowship is designed to help emerging reporters develop stronger skills in solutions-focused journalism, narrative complexity, civic reporting, climate solutions journalism, and community-centered storytelling. Selected fellows will receive practical training, editorial mentorship, opportunities to produce and publish original journalism, and a $1,000 stipend after successfully completing the program. The Fall 2026 cohort will run from September 14 to October 20, 2026. Applications must be submitted by August 3, 2026. The official announcement does not specify a deadline time or timezone, so applicants should avoid waiting until the final day to submit their materials. Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship at a Glance Program name: Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship Organizing publication: The Fulcrum Program initiative: The Fulcrum NextGen Fellowship period: September 14 to October 20, 2026 Application deadline: August 3, 2026 Financial support: $1,000 stipend upon completion Eligible academic levels: Undergraduate and graduate students Required field: Journalism Geographical eligibility: Students studying in the United States Academic requirement: Good academic standing Main training areas: Solutions journalism, narrative complexity, civic storytelling, community-centered reporting and climate solutions journalism Application method: Submission by email Publication opportunities: Original reporting may be published by The Fulcrum and the Latino News Network What Is the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship? The Fulcrum Fellowship is a journalism development program intended to support and train emerging reporters who want to move beyond simplistic, divisive or highly polarized news narratives. Rather than teaching fellows to report only on problems, conflict or institutional failures, the program introduces them to approaches that examine how individuals, communities and organizations are responding to public challenges. The purpose is not to avoid difficult subjects, but to report on those subjects with greater depth, evidence, context and human understanding. The Fall 2026 program will focus particularly on solutions-oriented reporting and the practice of “complicating the narrative.” This approach encourages reporters to look beyond two-sided political or social arguments and examine the experiences, motivations, contradictions and perspectives that are often missing from conventional reporting. Through these methods, fellows will learn how to produce journalism that presents communities as active participants in public life rather than merely as subjects affected by problems. The fellowship is part of The Fulcrum’s broader NextGen initiative, which seeks to increase opportunities for student journalists and strengthen youth participation in reporting about democracy, public policy, civic engagement and community renewal. First Fall Cohort of the Fulcrum Fellowship The 2026 program represents the first time that The Fulcrum is extending its fellowship program into the fall season. The expansion has been made possible through financial support from the Hortencia Zavala Foundation. The addition of a fall cohort creates another entry point for journalism students who may not have been able to participate in previous program cycles. The fall fellowship builds upon the experience of earlier cohorts and The Fulcrum’s ongoing work with student journalists. Previous initiatives have focused on preparing young reporters to cover complex social and political issues while avoiding narratives that reinforce polarization or reduce communities to stereotypes. By expanding the program, The Fulcrum aims to support more emerging journalists and strengthen the pipeline of reporters capable of producing responsible, constructive and community-focused public-interest journalism. Fellowship Dates and Program Duration The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship will take place from: September 14 to October 20, 2026 This means the program will operate for approximately six weeks. The announcement does not provide a detailed weekly schedule, session timetable or expected number of hours that fellows must contribute. Applicants with university classes, employment or other obligations should request clarification from the organizers if scheduling information is necessary before they apply. The relatively concentrated program period is expected to combine training, mentorship, reporting and the preparation of an original journalistic story. Main Objectives of the Fellowship The Fall Fulcrum Fellowship is intended to help participants: Develop practical skills in solutions-focused journalism. Understand how to report on complex political, civic and social issues without relying on oversimplified narratives. Produce original, evidence-based and community-centered reporting. Improve interviewing, research, writing and narrative-development skills. Explore how communities are responding to challenges affecting democracy and civic life. Receive professional guidance from experienced editors and journalism trainers. Build a stronger portfolio through potential publication. Develop experience in climate solutions journalism. Report more effectively across political, cultural and social differences. Prepare for future careers in newsrooms, nonprofit media, public-interest journalism and civic communication. Training in Solutions Journalism Solutions journalism is one of the central components of the fellowship. Under this approach, journalists investigate responses to social, environmental, political or economic problems. Reporting does not simply celebrate positive initiatives or promote organizations. Instead, it examines how a response works, what evidence supports it, what limitations it faces and whether lessons can be drawn from it. Fellows will be encouraged to look beyond descriptions of problems and investigate questions such as: Who is responding to the problem? What strategy or intervention is being used? What evidence shows whether the response is working? What challenges or limitations remain? Can the response provide lessons for other communities? Which voices are usually excluded from reporting on the issue? How can the story avoid exaggeration while still identifying meaningful progress? This training can help journalism students produce stories that are more informative and useful to communities while maintaining professional standards of verification and independence. Learning to “Complicate the Narrative” The program will also train participants in techniques commonly described as “complicating the narrative.” Traditional reporting frequently presents difficult public issues as conflicts between two opposing sides. Although this format can make a story appear simple, it may overlook important perspectives, internal disagreements, personal experiences and areas of common ground. Complicating the narrative involves asking deeper questions and recognizing that people’s beliefs and experiences do not always fit neatly into fixed political, cultural or social categories. For emerging journalists, this can involve: Interviewing people with different experiences and perspectives. Avoiding assumptions about communities or political groups. Exploring why individuals hold particular views. Identifying contradictions and changes in people’s opinions. Examining areas of agreement that may exist alongside disagreement. Including community voices that are often ignored. Reporting on people as individuals rather than political labels. Avoiding language that unnecessarily intensifies division. These skills can be particularly valuable when covering elections, public policy, immigration, education, climate change, local government, social inequality and other sensitive issues. Climate Solutions Journalism Component The Fall 2026 fellowship will include a climate solutions journalism component supported through sponsorship from the Solutions Journalism Network. Participants will receive training in reporting on responses to climate-related challenges and will produce an original story applying the methods introduced during the program. Climate journalism often focuses on disasters, environmental damage and scientific warnings. These issues remain essential, but solutions-focused reporting can also investigate how communities, institutions, researchers, businesses or governments are attempting to address climate risks. Potential reporting areas may include local climate adaptation, clean energy, sustainable transportation, water management, environmental justice, disaster preparedness, conservation, resilient infrastructure or community-led environmental initiatives. The fellowship announcement does not assign a specific climate topic in advance. Participants should therefore be prepared to identify, research and report an appropriate story in consultation with the program’s editors and mentors. Mentorship and Editorial Support Fellows will receive mentorship from members of the publication’s editorial team. Editorial mentorship is an important element of the program because it gives students an opportunity to develop their work with guidance from professionals who understand reporting, story development, verification, structure and publication standards. Depending on the needs of each story, mentorship may help fellows strengthen areas such as: Developing a clear and reportable story idea. Identifying relevant sources. Preparing effective interview questions. Examining evidence behind proposed solutions. Organizing complex information. Improving narrative flow. Distinguishing reporting from advocacy. Revising drafts in response to editorial feedback. Producing publication-ready journalism. The fellowship is led by Hugo Balta, publisher of the Latino News Network and executive editor of The Fulcrum. He is also an accredited solutions-journalism trainer with the Solutions Journalism Network and has previously served twice as president of the National Association of Hispanic Journalists. Opportunity to Publish Original Reporting Participants will have opportunities to produce and publish original journalism during the fellowship. The announcement states that fellows may have their work published through The Fulcrum. Their reporting may also appear on the Latino News Network, potentially allowing their stories to reach audiences interested in democracy, civic participation, public policy and community renewal. Publication can provide substantial professional value for journalism students. A published, professionally edited article can become part of a fellow’s reporting portfolio and may support future applications for internships, newsroom positions, graduate programs or additional fellowships. Applicants should understand that the opportunity is based on producing work that meets editorial and program requirements. The announcement does not guarantee that every draft will automatically be published without review or revision. $1,000 Fellowship Stipend Each fellow who completes the Fall 2026 program will receive a $1,000 stipend . The wording of the announcement indicates that payment is connected to completion of the fellowship. Participants should therefore be prepared to attend the required sessions, participate in the training process, work with mentors and complete the expected reporting assignment. The announcement does not provide further information about: The exact stipend payment date. Whether payment will be made in one installment. Tax treatment of the stipend. Whether any additional reporting expenses will be reimbursed. Whether fellows must provide specific tax or payment documentation. Applicants who require clarification regarding these financial arrangements should contact the fellowship organizers directly before accepting an offer. Who Is Eligible to Apply? The fellowship is open to: Undergraduate journalism students studying in the United States. Graduate journalism students studying in the United States. Students who are in good academic standing. The announcement does not mention a minimum grade-point average, age limit, graduation year, citizenship requirement or racial or ethnic restriction. It also does not clarify whether applicants must be enrolled in a degree formally titled “Journalism” or whether students in closely related fields such as communications, media studies or digital media will be considered. Students in related academic programs should contact the organizers for confirmation rather than assuming that they meet the field-of-study requirement. Application Documents Required Applicants must prepare the following four materials: Current Résumé The résumé should provide an updated overview of the applicant’s education, journalism experience, employment, internships, campus activities, volunteer work, technical abilities and relevant achievements. Students may include experience such as: Reporting for a university newspaper. Producing content for radio, television or digital media. Freelance writing. Podcasting. Documentary or multimedia production. Photography or video journalism. Community reporting. Journalism-related coursework. Research projects. Civic or public-policy engagement. Applicants should ensure that their contact information, educational status and experience are accurate and current. Examples of Published or Academic Work Applicants must submit examples of their work. These may include published journalism or relevant academic assignments. Suitable examples could include: News articles. Feature stories. Investigative reports. Multimedia journalism. Audio or video reports. University newspaper contributions. Class reporting assignments. Research-based academic writing. Community or civic reporting. Applicants should select samples that demonstrate clear writing, research ability, accuracy, curiosity and an understanding of journalistic storytelling. The announcement does not specify the number, length or preferred format of the samples. Applicants should therefore submit a focused selection of their strongest and most relevant work rather than sending an excessive number of documents. Cover Letter A cover letter must explain how participating in the fellowship would support the applicant’s professional and journalistic growth. A strong letter should directly address: The applicant’s interest in journalism. Relevant academic or reporting experience. Interest in solutions-focused reporting. Professional skills the applicant hopes to develop. Interest in democracy, civic engagement, public policy, community reporting or climate solutions. How mentorship and publication experience would support future goals. What perspective or experience the applicant would contribute to the cohort. The cover letter should be tailored specifically to the Fall Fulcrum Fellowship rather than using a generic internship or employment letter. One Letter of Recommendation Applicants must provide one recommendation letter. The announcement does not state who must write the recommendation. A suitable recommender may therefore be a journalism professor, academic adviser, editor, newsroom supervisor or another individual familiar with the applicant’s academic work, reporting ability and professional conduct. Applicants should approach their recommender early and provide sufficient time for the letter to be completed before the August 3 deadline. How to Apply for the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship There is no separate online application form mentioned in the announcement. Applications must be submitted by email. Click HERE . Follow these steps: Prepare an updated résumé. Select examples of published or academic work. Write a tailored cover letter explaining how the fellowship will support your growth. Obtain one letter of recommendation. Review all documents for accuracy and completeness. Attach the materials to an email. Use the required subject line: Fall Fulcrum Fellowship application Send the complete application to newsroom@fulcrum.us Submit the application no later than August 3, 2026. Applicants should confirm that all attachments open correctly and use clearly identifiable filenames, preferably including the applicant’s name and the document type. For example: Firstname-Lastname-Resume.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Cover-Letter.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Writing-Samples.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Recommendation.pdf All application requirements and the required email subject line should be followed exactly. Important Dates Application Deadline August 3, 2026 No submission time or timezone is included in the announcement. Early submission is therefore strongly recommended. Fellowship Start Date September 14, 2026 Fellowship End Date October 20, 2026 Applicants should ensure that they will be available throughout the program period before submitting an application. Why Journalism Students Should Consider Applying The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship offers more than a financial stipend. It provides emerging journalists with an opportunity to develop reporting skills that are increasingly relevant in a divided and rapidly changing media environment. Participants can benefit from: Professional journalism training. Direct editorial mentorship. Experience producing an original reported story. Exposure to solutions journalism methods. Training in climate solutions reporting. Opportunities for publication. Portfolio development. Experience covering civic and community issues. A stronger understanding of narrative complexity. A $1,000 completion stipend. The program may be especially valuable for students who want to report on democracy, public policy, climate challenges, civic participation and local community responses while avoiding superficial or unnecessarily polarized narratives. Tips for Preparing a Competitive Application Applicants should consider the following practical measures: Follow every instruction carefully. Missing documents or using an incorrect email subject line may weaken an otherwise strong application. Select relevant work samples. Submit pieces that demonstrate reporting, research, interviewing or evidence-based storytelling. Write a specific cover letter. Explain precisely how the fellowship connects to your experience and future journalism goals. Demonstrate curiosity about communities. The fellowship emphasizes human-centered and community-focused reporting. Show openness to editorial feedback. Mentorship and revision are central to professional journalism development. Mention relevant interests. Experience or interest in civic affairs, democracy, public policy, climate reporting or solutions journalism can strengthen the application when presented honestly. Contact a recommender immediately. Recommendation letters frequently require more time than applicants expect. Proofread the complete submission. Check names, dates, links, attachments, formatting and contact details. Apply before the deadline. The official announcement specifically encourages interested students to apply early. Conclusion The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship is a valuable paid opportunity for undergraduate and graduate journalism students in the United States who want to strengthen their reporting, writing and storytelling abilities. Through training in solutions journalism, narrative complexity, climate reporting and community-centered storytelling, participants will gain practical tools for covering difficult public issues with greater evidence, depth and accuracy. Fellows will also receive professional mentorship, produce original reporting, gain potential publication exposure through The Fulcrum and the Latino News Network, and receive a $1,000 stipend after completing the program. Eligible journalism students should begin preparing their résumé, work samples, cover letter and recommendation letter as soon as possible. Complete applications must be emailed to newsroom@fulcrum.us with the subject line “Fall Fulcrum Fellowship application” by August 3, 2026 . For more global opportunities, click HERE . title: Senior Policy Manager at J-PAL Europe in Zurich, Switzerland | Work with Nobel Laureates on Evidence-Based Public Policy
summary: Are you an experienced public policy professional passionate about transforming rigorous research into real-world policy impact? The Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL) Europe is seeking a Senior Policy Manager to join its team in Zurich, Switzerland . This prestigious opportunity allows the successful candidate to work directly with J-PAL co-founders and Nobel Prize-winning economists Professor Abhijit Banerjee and Professor Esther Duflo , supporting their global engagement with governments, multilateral organizations, philanthropic foundations, and development partners. The role is ideal for experienced professionals who have built strong careers in public policy, international development, economics, or related sectors and are eager to influence evidence-informed policymaking across Europe and beyond. About J-PAL Europe The Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL) is a globally recognized research center dedicated to reducing poverty through evidence-based policymaking. Anchored by a network of more than 2,100 affiliated researchers at universities around the world, J-PAL works to ensure that public policies and social programs are informed by rigorous scientific research. J-PAL Europe is one of the organization s seven regional offices and is hosted by the Paris School of Economics . The office leads initiatives across Europe to promote the use of high-quality evidence in policymaking while collaborating with governments, international organizations, and development agencies. About the Position The Senior Policy Manager will be based in Zurich, Switzerland , and will work directly with Professors Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo . Reporting jointly to the Global Executive Directors of J-PAL and the Executive Director of J-PAL Europe, the successful candidate will oversee strategic partnerships, manage high-level external engagements, support fundraising activities, and help translate academic research into accessible policy recommendations. Earliest Start Date: 1 September 2026 Latest Start Date: 1 September 2026 Key Responsibilities The successful candidate will play a central role in advancing J-PAL s mission through several core responsibilities. Strategic Partnership Management Responsibilities include: Managing partnership and collaboration requests directed to J-PAL s founders Reviewing and prioritizing incoming opportunities Assessing strategic alignment of partnership requests Building relationships with: European governments Foundations Development agencies Multilateral organizations Supporting long-term partnerships with European institutions Representing J-PAL during conferences and senior stakeholder meetings Policy Communication and Knowledge Dissemination The Senior Policy Manager will: Prepare policy briefs Write evidence summaries Develop speeches and opinion articles Produce blogs and communication materials Translate complex economic research into accessible policy messages Ensure scientific accuracy across communication outputs Support dissemination activities across the global J-PAL network Fundraising and Strategic Development Key duties include: Identifying funding opportunities Developing grant proposals Preparing concept notes Building relationships with current and prospective donors Supporting the strategic growth of J-PAL Europe s policy portfolio Coordination and Leadership The role also involves: Coordinating with J-PAL Europe and Global leadership Working closely with regional offices and affiliated researchers Managing external engagement activities Supporting policy training initiatives Contributing to organizational priorities Undertaking regular international travel to locations including: Zurich Paris Cambridge Other partner countries Professional Development Opportunities J-PAL invests heavily in staff development. Successful candidates will benefit from: Learn Free access to MIT s MicroMasters in Data, Economics, and Development Policy (DEDP) Continuous professional learning Collaboration with leading economists Engage Work directly with Nobel Prize-winning researchers Interact with policymakers worldwide Help shape evidence-based policies Travel Attend international conferences Meet government officials Collaborate with donor organizations Participate in policy events throughout Europe Grow Receive mentorship Share best practices across J-PAL s international network Build expertise in evidence-informed policymaking Eligibility Criteria Applicants should possess: Education Master s degree in: Public Policy Economics International Development Or another closely related discipline Professional Experience Candidates must have: At least 8 years of relevant professional experience Experience working with governments, multilateral organizations, foundations, or development agencies Demonstrated fundraising experience Experience writing successful grant proposals Excellent stakeholder management skills Technical Knowledge Applicants should have: Familiarity with randomized impact evaluations Understanding of evidence-informed policymaking Ability to communicate research findings clearly Knowledge of climate or environmental impact evaluations (desirable) Language Requirements Required: Fluency in English Preferred: Professional fluency in German Professional fluency in French Knowledge of both German and French will be a significant advantage. Desired Skills J-PAL seeks professionals who demonstrate: Exceptional writing ability Strong analytical thinking Strategic decision-making Excellent organizational skills Ability to manage multiple priorities Strong interpersonal communication Experience working across different countries and cultures Leadership and mentoring abilities Passion for evidence-based policymaking Work Authorization Applicants must already have authorization to work in Switzerland . Visa sponsorship is not available for this position. How to Apply Interested applicants should complete the J-PAL/IPA Common Application and: Select the Policy job category Choose Senior Policy Manager – J-PAL Europe – Zurich Upload: Updated CV Cover Letter Include details regarding: Relevant professional experience Qualifications Authorization to work in Switzerland Applications should be submitted no later than August 16, 2026 . Why You Should Apply This is an exceptional opportunity to contribute to one of the world s leading organizations dedicated to evidence-based policymaking. Working alongside Nobel Prize-winning economists Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo, the successful candidate will help shape policies that improve lives around the world. Professionals interested in public policy, international development, economics, strategic partnerships, and research communication will find this role both intellectually rewarding and globally impactful. Application Portal Application Portal: https://bit.ly/4vEUXGX Application Dead line: August 16, 2026 Conclusion The Senior Policy Manager position at J-PAL Europe offers experienced policy professionals the chance to work at the highest levels of international development and evidence-informed policymaking. Based in Zurich, the role combines strategic leadership, partnership management, fundraising, policy communication, and collaboration with world-renowned researchers to influence public policy across Europe and beyond. If you have the required experience, outstanding communication skills, and authorization to work in Switzerland, this is a unique opportunity to make a lasting global impact. title: Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026: Fully Funded Opportunity for 100 Young Leaders to Participate in the UN Water Conference in Abu Dhabi
summary: Applications are now open for the Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026 , an international leadership and capacity-building initiative that will prepare 100 outstanding young leaders from around the world to actively contribute to global water governance and participate in the 2026 United Nations Water Conference in Abu Dhabi. Established by the Arab Youth Center in strategic partnership with the United Arab Emirates Ministry of Foreign Affairs , the program is designed to ensure that youth are not merely observers of global decision-making processes but meaningful contributors to international water policy discussions and solutions. The initiative is being launched ahead of the 2026 UN Water Conference , which will be co-hosted by the United Arab Emirates and the Republic of Senegal in Abu Dhabi this December and has received endorsement from the Conference Presidencies. Rather than limiting young people to symbolic participation, the Global Youth Water Envoys Program places selected participants at the center of international dialogue, equipping them with the knowledge, skills, mentorship, and opportunities needed to engage directly with policymakers, national delegations, experts, and institutional partners working to address one of the world s most urgent challenges—water security. About the Global Youth Water Envoys Program The Global Youth Water Envoys Program is an intensive leadership development initiative that combines capacity building, diplomatic engagement, policy dialogue, and international collaboration. The program will select 100 young leaders representing different regions of the world and guide them through a structured learning journey that includes: Foundational training Advanced thematic specialization Leadership development Policy engagement Direct interaction with governments and international organizations Participation in the UN Water Conference The program aims to create a new generation of youth leaders capable of influencing water governance at national, regional, and international levels. Why the Program Matters Water insecurity continues to be one of the greatest development challenges facing the world. Millions of people—particularly across developing countries—continue to experience water scarcity, poor sanitation, climate-related water challenges, and unequal access to safe water resources. Communities throughout the Global South are disproportionately affected by these challenges, while young people often remain underrepresented in formal water governance and international policy-making processes. The Global Youth Water Envoys Program seeks to bridge this gap by creating a structured platform where youth expertise, innovation, lived experiences, and policy contributions directly influence global discussions and international cooperation. The initiative reflects the United Arab Emirates commitment to: Strengthening international partnerships. Promoting regional cooperation. Empowering youth leadership. Supporting sustainable development. Advancing innovative solutions for global water challenges. Program Structure Selected participants will progress through several phases designed to prepare them for meaningful engagement at the global level. These include: Comprehensive online learning. Capacity-building sessions. Expert-led workshops. Specialized thematic training. Interactive dialogue preparation. Diplomatic engagement. Networking with institutional partners. Participation in international policy discussions. Throughout the program, participants will work alongside experts and policymakers while strengthening their leadership, negotiation, communication, and collaboration skills. Responsibilities of Global Youth Water Envoys Participants selected for the program will be expected to actively engage throughout the entire program. Their responsibilities include: Completing all required training sessions. Participating in engagement activities. Dedicating approximately 3–4 hours each week throughout the active program period. Contributing to their assigned Interactive Dialogue. Demonstrating professionalism, collaboration, and respect. Representing youth voices during discussions on global water governance. Participating in major program milestones. Taking part in the 2026 UN Water Conference , subject to logistical arrangements. Engaging with policymakers, experts, institutional leaders, and international organizations. Supporting discussions across the Conference s six Interactive Dialogues . Promoting knowledge exchange and long-term policy development. What Participants Will Gain The program offers exceptional professional and leadership development opportunities. Successful applicants will: Join an international network of 100 Global Youth Water Envoys . Receive world-class training from leading water sector experts. Benefit from mentorship throughout the program. Contribute to developing the Global Youth Water Statement . Build professional relationships with policymakers and international organizations. Expand their international professional network. Participate in the 2026 UN Water Conference in Abu Dhabi. Become members of an international alumni community. Access future collaboration and engagement opportunities after completing the program. Who Should Apply? The program welcomes applications from passionate young people interested in solving global water challenges and contributing to international policy discussions. Applicants should: Be between 18 and 35 years old . Demonstrate proficiency in English, the official working language of the program. Have an interest in or experience related to: Water governance Public policy Climate action Sustainability Innovation Finance Environmental development Other water-related disciplines Be committed to completing the entire program. Be willing to represent youth voices within their communities and internationally. Selection is based on merit, with priority given to applicants from water-stressed regions identified by the Water Stress Country Index, particularly: Africa Arab States Asia and the Pacific Nevertheless, applications from young leaders around the world are encouraged. Selection Process The Global Youth Water Envoys Program is committed to ensuring broad geographic representation while maintaining a competitive merit-based selection process. The organizers seek participants who demonstrate: Leadership potential. Passion for sustainable development. Commitment to water security. Interest in international cooperation. Ability to contribute meaningfully to policy discussions. Application Deadline The deadline to submit applications is: 13 August 2026 (12:00 AM Abu Dhabi Time). Interested applicants are encouraged to complete their applications well before the deadline to avoid last-minute technical issues. Apply HERE Applicants can learn more about the Global Youth Water Envoys Program HERE Conclusion The Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026 presents a remarkable opportunity for young leaders worldwide to become active contributors to international water governance and sustainable development. Through expert training, mentorship, diplomatic engagement, and direct participation in the 2026 UN Water Conference, participants will gain the knowledge, networks, and experience needed to shape global conversations on one of humanity s most pressing challenges. If you are passionate about water security, climate action, public policy, or sustainable development, this program offers an outstanding platform to amplify your voice and make a lasting impact. Be sure to submit your application before the deadline of 13 August 2026 to be considered for this prestigious global initiative. title: Encore Fellowships in the United States | $25,000 Paid Fellowship for Experienced Professionals
summary: Are you an experienced professional looking to use your expertise to create meaningful social impact? The Encore Fellowships program offers accomplished professionals an opportunity to transition their decades of experience into nonprofit leadership while earning a $25,000 stipend . Designed for professionals with at least 20 years of work experience , Encore Fellowships connect skilled individuals with nonprofit organizations that need experienced leaders to strengthen their operations, build organizational capacity, and expand their impact. Fellows work on strategic projects while gaining valuable exposure to the nonprofit sector and making a lasting difference in their communities. If you are passionate about using your professional background for meaningful change, the Encore Fellowship could be the ideal next step in your career. About Encore Fellowships Encore Fellowships are designed to bridge the gap between experienced professionals and nonprofit organizations seeking high-level expertise. Rather than filling traditional employment positions, Fellows are matched with organizations that require strategic guidance, leadership, and specialized skills for capacity-building initiatives. Each Fellow works closely with a nonprofit organization over a period of 6 to 12 months , contributing approximately 1,000 hours —typically around 20 hours per week , although some placements may require up to 35 hours weekly. Participants receive professional support while helping nonprofit organizations strengthen their operations and increase long-term sustainability. Fellowship Benefits Selected Fellows will receive numerous professional and personal benefits, including: $25,000 compensation for their service Opportunity to work on high-impact nonprofit projects Collaborate directly with nonprofit leadership teams Apply decades of professional expertise to meaningful causes Expand knowledge of the nonprofit and social impact sector Develop new leadership experiences Build professional networks within mission-driven organizations Flexible work arrangements depending on placement Remote Hybrid Onsite Fellowship Structure Successful Fellows will: Contribute approximately 1,000 hours Serve for 6–12 months Work approximately 20–35 hours each week Support nonprofit organizations on strategic capacity-building projects Be employed and paid through Encore Fellowships, which manages: Candidate screening Onboarding Payroll administration Employment logistics Eligibility Criteria Applicants must meet the following requirements: Have 20 or more years of paid professional work experience Demonstrate a strong interest in supporting nonprofit or social impact organizations Be able to commit approximately 1,000 hours over 6–12 months Be legally eligible to work in the United States Be available to work remotely, onsite, or in a hybrid arrangement Possess significant professional expertise that can benefit nonprofit organizations Ideal Candidates Encore Fellowships are particularly suitable for professionals who have served in senior or leadership positions such as: Executive Directors Marketing Directors Communications Directors Digital Transformation Leaders Operations Managers Human Resource Leaders Finance Professionals IT Specialists Program Managers Strategy Consultants Business Executives Project Managers Professionals from both the private and public sectors are encouraged to express their interest. Areas of Expertise Sought Encore Fellowships welcome professionals with expertise in areas including: Marketing Communications Brand Strategy Fundraising Digital Marketing Analytics Social Media Technology Digital Transformation Human Resources Finance Operations Project Management Leadership Development Organizational Strategy Capacity Building Work Location Options Depending on the nonprofit organization s needs, Fellows may work: Remotely Onsite Hybrid Who Can Apply? Applications are currently open to experienced professionals who: Reside in the United States Are eligible to work in the United States Meet the professional experience requirements At this time, the program is only available in the United States . Application Process Interested applicants should complete the online interest form and provide: Personal contact information Work experience Preferred work location LinkedIn profile Previous leadership positions Areas of expertise Previous industry experience Additional comments about their interests Following submission, an Encore Fellowship Manager will contact eligible candidates to discuss suitable fellowship opportunities. Required Information Applicants should prepare: First and last name Telephone number Email address City and State ZIP Code LinkedIn Profile Years of paid work experience Previous job titles Core professional expertise Industry background Work location preference General comments Why Apply? Encore Fellowships provide a unique opportunity to transition decades of professional experience into meaningful social impact. Instead of stepping away from leadership after a successful career, Fellows continue making a difference by helping nonprofit organizations solve real-world challenges. The fellowship allows professionals to use their expertise in strategy, operations, communications, technology, finance, and many other fields while receiving financial compensation and expanding their professional networks. It is an excellent opportunity for experienced professionals who want to leave a lasting legacy through service. How to Apply Interested applicants should complete the official Encore Fellowships interest form. Application Portal: https://fedcap.tfaforms.net/5178761?tfa_Ownerid=005G000000641Ng tfa_9504653290883=001Qn00000SxnZQ For questions regarding the application process: Email: efinfo@fedcap.org Conclusion The Encore Fellowships program offers experienced professionals an exceptional opportunity to leverage decades of expertise in service of nonprofit organizations across the United States. With a $25,000 paid fellowship , flexible work arrangements, and meaningful capacity-building projects, the program enables accomplished professionals to continue making a significant impact while supporting organizations dedicated to social good. If you have over 20 years of professional experience and are passionate about giving back through your skills and leadership, this fellowship is well worth exploring. title: Institute for Youth in Policy Public Policy Fellowship 2026 (Fall Cohort): Apply for a Selective U.S. Leadership Programme in Public Policy, Research & Civic Engagement
summary: Applications are open for the Institute for Youth in Policy Public Policy Fellowship 2026 Fall Cohort , an intensive two-month development programme for young people interested in public policy, civic engagement and social impact. The fellowship gives selected students opportunities to examine contemporary political, social, economic and environmental issues while developing practical skills in policy research, analysis, writing, communication, teamwork and project management. Fellows participate in seminars, debates, workshops, speaker sessions and collaborative policy activities. They also develop an original individual capstone project and work with peers on a nonpartisan policy brief. Applications for the Fall 2026 cohort close on 19 August 2026 . Decisions are expected to be released on 26 August 2026 , while the official programme website lists the cohort dates as 12 September to 7 November 2026 . About the Public Policy Fellowship The Public Policy Fellowship is organised by the Institute for Youth in Policy , a youth-focused organisation working in public policy, civic education, discourse and political engagement. The programme is designed to help emerging leaders understand how public policy is researched, developed, communicated and debated. Rather than focusing exclusively on lectures, the fellowship combines academic learning with collaborative and practical assignments. Participants explore policy from personal, local and broader national or global perspectives. They work with fellow students, programme mentors, YIP department executives, guest speakers and other stakeholders to examine current policy challenges and propose evidence-informed responses. Duration and Fall 2026 Dates The fellowship runs for approximately two months. The official 2026 schedule lists four cohorts: Winter: 17 January–21 March 2026 Spring: 11 April–6 June 2026 Summer: 20 June–22 August 2026 Fall: 12 September–7 November 2026 The Fall cohort application deadline is 19 August 2026 , and admissions decisions are scheduled for 26 August 2026 . Applications close at 11:59 p.m. PST on the stated deadline date. A recent promotional announcement states that the Fall cohort ends on 8 November. However, the official fellowship webpage currently lists 7 November 2026 . Applicants should follow the dates communicated in their acceptance materials if selected. What Fellows Will Learn The fellowship is structured to help young participants develop skills relevant to public policy, civic leadership, research and advocacy. Participants can expect to strengthen their abilities in: Public policy research Policy analysis Nonpartisan thinking Research and analytical writing Strategic communication Public speaking and presentation Debate and constructive discourse Teamwork Project management Problem-solving Leadership Community and stakeholder engagement The programme encourages fellows to consider complex policy problems from different perspectives rather than approaching them through a single political or ideological position. Programme Activities Selected fellows participate in several interconnected activities throughout the programme. Interactive Seminars and Workshops Fellows attend sessions designed to introduce policy concepts, research methods and approaches to civic engagement. Workshops may focus on subjects such as policy development, political communication, social impact, advocacy, research writing and project planning. Policy Discussions and Debates Thought-provoking discussions allow participants to examine policy challenges, hear different perspectives and practise communicating their ideas clearly. The emphasis on nonpartisan discussion is intended to help fellows engage respectfully with disagreement and analyse issues beyond political slogans. Guest Speaker Sessions Each cohort normally includes two speaker sessions with professionals or experts working in different policy fields. These sessions give fellows opportunities to learn about real-world policy work and ask questions about public service, advocacy, leadership and research. Collaborative Policy Brief Fellows work with their peers to research, write and present an original nonpartisan policy brief. This assignment provides practical experience in: Identifying a policy problem Conducting background research Evaluating evidence Comparing possible policy responses Developing recommendations Writing for a policy audience Presenting findings clearly Individual Capstone Project Every fellow is also expected to develop an individual capstone project. The capstone begins during the early part of the programme and continues until the end of the fellowship. Completed projects may be published on the Institute for Youth in Policy website. Previous fellowship topics have addressed areas such as social change, education, public-policy reform, urban renewal and technology-related initiatives. Weekly Schedule and Time Commitment The programme is designed to accommodate students with school, university and other responsibilities. According to the official fellowship page, participants attend two mandatory synchronous meetings each week: Wednesdays at 8:00 p.m. EST Saturdays at 12:00 p.m. EST Fellows generally spend approximately four to six hours per week on meetings, research, policy writing, workshops and independent capstone work. Applicants should convert the meeting times carefully and confirm the applicable time zone when the programme begins, particularly because seasonal clock changes can affect local meeting times. Policy Areas Covered The fellowship takes a cross-sector approach. Participants may examine issues connected to: Social and political change Education Criminal justice Economic policy Environmental policy Urban renewal Public-policy reform Community development Technology and SMART infrastructure Civic engagement Advocacy Political institutions The exact subjects may vary according to the cohort, speakers, current events and fellows’ capstone interests. Mentorship and Professional Development Fellows receive ongoing guidance throughout the programme. Mentorship and small-group support can help participants improve their research, refine their policy arguments and develop clearer professional goals. The fellowship also provides opportunities to connect with: Other students interested in policy YIP programme leaders Experienced mentors Policy and education professionals Fellowship alumni Guest speakers The Institute reports that more than 1,000 fellows have graduated, over 300 capstones have been published and fellowship projects have received approximately 3.2 million views. It also reports an average acceptance rate of 5–8%, indicating a selective admissions process. What Happens After the Fellowship? Completing the programme may provide participants with a published research or policy project that can be added to an academic or professional portfolio. Fellowship graduates may also receive priority consideration for selected positions within the Institute for Youth in Policy. The organisation states that alumni can access a priority hiring pipeline and opportunities to connect with senior YIP leadership. However, completing the fellowship does not guarantee employment or appointment to another YIP position. Who Can Apply? The official fellowship website currently states that applicants must be: High school or undergraduate students Between 13 and 23 years old Interested or experienced in public policy, civic engagement, advocacy, social impact, community organising, design thinking or constructive discourse Based in the United States Able to participate in meetings scheduled around Eastern, Central and Pacific U.S. time zones Graduate students and people who have already graduated are not eligible. The official website also states that the programme is not open to international applicants because the workshops focus substantially on U.S. public policy and political systems. Important Eligibility Discrepancy A recent promotional post describes the Fall fellowship as open to students aged 16–25 and refers to collaboration with peers and mentors from around the world. However, the official fellowship page currently states: Ages 13–23 High school and undergraduate students only No graduate students or graduates No international applicants Because these details conflict, prospective applicants should rely on the current official application form or contact the fellowship team directly at fellowship@yipinstitute.org before applying, especially if they are aged 24–25 or based outside the United States. How to Apply Applicants must complete the online fellowship application form. The application will likely ask candidates to explain their interests, background, motivation and experience related to policy or civic engagement. Applicants should prepare to discuss: Why they are interested in public policy A policy issue they care about Their experience in leadership, advocacy or community engagement What they hope to learn How they would contribute to the cohort Their ability to meet the weekly time commitment APPLY HERE Tips for a Strong Application A strong application should be focused, specific and personal. Applicants should: Identify one or two policy issues they genuinely care about. Explain why those issues matter to their community or personal experience. Provide examples of leadership, research, volunteering or civic participation. Demonstrate curiosity and willingness to engage with different perspectives. Explain how the fellowship supports their academic or career goals. Avoid general statements that could apply to every candidate. Proofread the application before submission. Candidates do not necessarily need an extensive policy résumé. A thoughtful explanation of their interests, initiative and potential contribution may be more persuasive than a parade of impressive-sounding titles wearing tiny hats. Application Deadline Applications for the Fall Public Policy Fellowship close on: 19 August 2026 at 11:59 p.m. PST Admissions decisions are scheduled to be released on: 26 August 2026 The official Fall cohort dates are: 12 September–7 November 2026 Applicants should submit early and verify that they have received confirmation that their application was successfully recorded. Conclusion The Institute for Youth in Policy Public Policy Fellowship 2026 offers high school and undergraduate students an opportunity to gain practical experience in policy research, analytical writing, debate, communication and civic leadership. Through workshops, mentorship, collaborative policy briefs, speaker events and an individual capstone project, fellows can develop tangible work while joining a selective community of young people interested in public affairs. Eligible students should carefully review the official requirements, confirm that they can attend the two weekly meetings and submit their applications by 19 August 2026 at 11:59 p.m. PST . For more information about this opportunity, CLICK HERE . To discover more fellowship opportunities, CLICK HERE .
