title: GEF SGP Nigeria Call for Proposals 2026: Strategic Grant to Host the Western & Eastern Anglophone Africa Regional Capacity-Building Workshop
summary: GEF SGP Nigeria is inviting qualified organisations to submit proposals to organise and deliver the Western and Eastern Anglophone Africa Regional Capacity-Building Workshop 2026. The regional workshop is expected to take place in Abuja, Nigeria, in November 2026 and bring together approximately 35 participants from participating African countries. Attendees are expected to include National Coordinators, former and current grantees, National Steering Committee representatives, UNDP Country Office representatives, the SGP Regional Focal Point and members of the SGP Central Programme Management Team. The selected organisation will manage the workshop’s travel, accommodation, subsistence, conference arrangements, field visit, visibility materials and knowledge products. The grant provides funding of up to US$100,000 , while applicants must demonstrate secured co-financing of at least the same amount in cash or in kind. The deadline for submitting proposals is 27 July 2026 . About the GEF SGP Regional Workshop The Global Environment Facility (GEF) Small Grants Programme (SGP) supports community-led initiatives that address environmental challenges while contributing to sustainable development. The planned workshop will provide a regional platform for countries participating in the Small Grants Programme to examine results achieved during Operational Phase 7 and prepare for continued implementation under Operational Phase 8. Participants will discuss achievements, challenges, lessons learned, impact stories and future opportunities for collaboration and resource mobilisation. The call states that participants will come from 12 countries, but the announcement lists 11: Cape Verde Eritrea Ethiopia The Gambia Ghana Liberia Nigeria Rwanda Sierra Leone Tanzania Uganda Prospective applicants should consult the official proposal documents for clarification regarding the final list of participating countries. Main Objectives of the Regional Workshop The Western and Eastern Anglophone Africa Regional Capacity-Building Workshop is intended to support reflection, learning and future planning across participating GEF SGP country programmes. Its main objectives include the following. Review Operational Phase 7 Implementation Participants will review progress under Operational Phase 7, including: Landscape approaches Country-level targets Results achieved Challenges encountered Lessons learned Good practices developed at country and project levels Environmental and community impact stories This review will help participants identify what worked, what needs improvement and what lessons should guide future programme implementation. Discuss GEF-8 Priorities The workshop will create space for National Coordinators and other stakeholders to discuss the strategic objectives and wider vision of GEF-8. These discussions are expected to help participating countries understand how their national and regional activities connect with the current priorities of the Global Environment Facility. Assess the Transition to Operational Phase 8 Participants will review progress concerning the transition to UNDP and implementation under Operational Phase 8. The discussions may cover: Operational arrangements Administrative processes Grant-making procedures Common implementation problems Potential regional solutions Opportunities for improved coordination Promote Peer Learning A field visit to a selected SGP-supported project will allow participants to observe community-level implementation and exchange practical experience. The visit is expected to support peer learning by showing how environmental initiatives are managed in practice and how communities contribute to project results. Explore Future Resource Mobilisation The workshop will examine opportunities to strengthen the long-term sustainability of the Small Grants Programme. Topics are expected to include: GEF-9 STAR negotiations at country level Partnerships with the private sector Resource mobilisation Replication of successful initiatives Scaling up effective projects Transboundary initiatives across the African region Sustainability of community-based environmental action Strengthen Communication of SGP Results Another major objective is to improve how the programme communicates its results and impact. The selected organisation will support the production of a documentary and publication highlighting projects from participating countries. These materials should present: Project results Impact stories Lessons learned Good practices Community experiences Examples of successful environmental interventions Scope of Work for the Selected Organisation The successful organisation will be responsible for coordinating the workshop before, during and after the event. 1. Organise the Regional Workshop in Abuja The organisation will plan and deliver one Africa subregional workshop for approximately 35 participants. The grant is expected to cover: International and domestic airfares Accommodation Subsistence costs Conference packages Workshop venue arrangements Participant coordination Pre-event logistics On-site logistical support Post-event follow-up The selected organisation will work with the SGP Nigeria office throughout the planning and implementation process. 2. Organise a Project Site Visit The organisation must arrange a visit to an SGP-selected project. Relevant costs may include: Transport hire Accommodation where required Meals and subsistence Coordination with the host project Participant logistics Field-visit documentation 3. Produce a Video Documentary A video documentary must be produced to record the project site visit and communicate the work and impact of the Small Grants Programme. The documentary should help present community-level achievements in a format that can be used for learning, visibility and outreach. 4. Produce a Regional Publication The organisation will prepare a publication featuring projects presented by participating countries. The publication should document: Project results Environmental and community impact Lessons learned Individual project case studies Replicable practices Examples of innovation Regional experiences under Operational Phase 7 5. Produce Workshop Materials The selected organisation will be responsible for producing relevant workshop and visibility materials, including: T-shirts Pens Jackets Reusable or biodegradable bags Other approved workshop materials Where possible, materials should reflect environmental sustainability principles. 6. Prepare the Final Workshop Report A comprehensive report must be produced following the workshop. The report should capture: Workshop proceedings Main discussion points Presentations Lessons learned Recommendations Agreements and action points Field-visit findings Opportunities for future collaboration Complete reports must be submitted no later than two weeks after the regional workshop. Expected Outputs The successful applicant will be expected to deliver the following outputs: Africa subregional workshop report Regional publication of GEF SGP impact stories Individual project case studies Video documentary covering the project site visit Workshop and visibility materials Evidence of successful workshop implementation Documentation of lessons, recommendations and follow-up actions Maximum Grant Amount The maximum grant available is: Up to US$100,000 The final amount awarded may depend on the quality of the proposal, approved activities, budget justification and the assessment of the applicant’s capacity. Applicants should prepare a realistic and detailed budget covering the activities required under the terms of reference. Co-Financing Requirement Applicants must provide evidence of secured co-financing worth at least: US$100,000 The contribution may be provided in: Cash In-kind support A combination of cash and in-kind contributions Applicants should clearly explain the source, amount and status of each contribution. In-kind contributions may include eligible staff time, equipment, facilities, services or other resources directly supporting programme delivery, subject to the official rules. A vague promise of future support is unlikely to be enough. The call specifically requires evidence that commensurate co-financing has been secured. Project Duration The project will run for four months , beginning in July 2026. The regional workshop itself is expected to take place in November 2026. Final reports must be submitted no later than two weeks after the workshop. Applicants should provide a practical implementation plan covering: Preparatory activities Participant and travel coordination Workshop delivery Field-visit arrangements Knowledge-product development Reporting and financial closure Eligibility Criteria Applicants must satisfy all the stated eligibility requirements. Eligible organisations must: Be previous GEF Small Grants Programme grantees Have experience organising international workshops Demonstrate experience in strategic knowledge management Have experience managing SGP grants valued at no less than US$50,000 Demonstrate experience in partnership building Demonstrate experience in resource mobilisation Have sufficient organisational and financial capacity Be able to secure the required co-financing Be able to complete all activities and reports within the project period The announcement explicitly advises organisations not to contact the programme unless they meet every listed criterion. What Could Strengthen an Application? A competitive proposal should provide strong evidence rather than broad claims. Applicants should consider including: Examples of international workshops previously organised References from former partners Evidence of completed SGP-funded projects Financial records showing experience managing grants above US$50,000 Examples of publications, documentaries or knowledge products A clear logistics plan A detailed risk-management strategy Confirmed co-financing documentation A realistic work plan Relevant staff biographies Evidence of partnership-building experience A clear approach to environmental sustainability The budget should be consistent with the work plan and should clearly distinguish grant-funded costs from co-financing. How to Apply Interested and eligible organisations should request the official proposal template by contacting: ibironke-favour.olubamise@undp.org Applicants should use the required template and follow all instructions provided by the GEF SGP Nigeria office. Only shortlisted organisations will be contacted. Application Deadline The deadline for submitting proposals is: 27 July 2026 The announcement does not specify a closing time or time zone. Applicants should submit before the final day to avoid uncertainty or technical delays. Conclusion The GEF SGP Nigeria Call for Proposals 2026 offers an experienced former Small Grants Programme grantee the opportunity to organise a major regional capacity-building workshop in Abuja. The selected organisation will manage a complex assignment involving international travel, accommodation, conference logistics, a field visit, documentary production, regional publication development and final reporting. With grant funding of up to US$100,000 and a matching co-financing requirement of at least US$100,000, applicants must demonstrate significant institutional experience, financial capacity and knowledge-management expertise. Eligible organisations should request the official proposal template, prepare a detailed application and submit it by 27 July 2026 . For further information and application requirements, CLICK HERE. To discover more grants opportunities, VISIT HERE. The post GEF SGP Nigeria Call for Proposals 2026: Strategic Grant to Host the Western Eastern Anglophone Africa Regional Capacity-Building Workshop appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027: Paid Finance Internship in Washington, DC with Visa Sponsorship for International and U.S. College Students
summary: The World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027 is now accepting applications from outstanding undergraduate students who aspire to build careers in finance, investment banking, capital markets, treasury operations, asset management, sustainable finance, and international development. As the only internship programme within the World Bank dedicated specifically to college students , this highly competitive opportunity provides participants with practical experience at the intersection of global finance, innovation, and development. Hosted by the World Bank Treasury , the internship offers far more than a traditional summer work experience. Interns become part of one of the world s most respected public-sector financial institutions, working alongside experts responsible for managing hundreds of billions of dollars in assets, supporting global financial markets, and financing development projects that improve lives worldwide. Throughout the 10-week programme, participants rotate across multiple Treasury teams, receive mentorship from experienced professionals, attend technical and career development sessions, network with senior leaders, and complete a capstone project. The internship also serves as the primary recruitment pathway into the prestigious World Bank Treasury Junior Analyst Programme , making it an outstanding opportunity for students planning long-term careers in finance. Applications for the World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027 are currently open. About the World Bank Treasury The World Bank Treasury (WBG Treasury) is the treasury department of the World Bank Group and serves as one of the world s leading public-sector treasury organizations. Reporting directly to the Managing Director and World Bank Group Chief Financial Officer, Treasury performs treasury and financial management functions for: International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) International Development Association (IDA) International Finance Corporation (IFC) Other World Bank Group entities Selected public- and private-sector organizations For more than 80 years, Treasury has earned a reputation as a prudent borrower, innovative investor, and trusted risk manager in international financial markets. Its responsibilities include: Managing approximately US$300 billion in assets for the World Bank Group and other official-sector investors. Leading the Triple-A-rated financing programmes of IBRD, IDA, and IFC in international and domestic bond and derivative markets. Conducting asset and liability management for the balance sheets of IBRD, IDA, and IFC. Developing financing and hedging products for developing-country clients. Supporting World Bank member countries through advisory services that strengthen financial market capacity. Working closely with finance, operations, investment, and development teams across the World Bank Group. This broad scope gives interns unparalleled exposure to global financial markets while contributing to the World Bank s mission of supporting sustainable development. About the World Bank Group The World Bank Group is one of the world s largest sources of development finance, technical expertise, and research. The organization consists of five institutions working together to reduce poverty, promote shared prosperity, and support sustainable development across the globe. Key facts include: 189 member countries More than 120 offices worldwide Partnerships with governments, international organizations, and private-sector institutions Investments in projects that address some of the world s most pressing development challenges By joining the Treasury Summer Internship, participants gain insight into how global finance contributes to economic growth and development. About the World Bank Treasury Summer Internship The World Bank Treasury Summer Internship is a junior professional training programme designed specifically for college students with strong academic records and a demonstrated interest in finance. The programme prepares students for careers in: Capital markets Investment banking Asset management Treasury operations Financial technology Sustainable finance International finance Development finance Risk management Unlike many internships that focus on observation, participants actively contribute to Treasury projects while receiving structured professional training and mentorship. The internship combines: Hands-on work experience Treasury team rotations Technical finance training Leadership development Networking opportunities Executive engagement Capstone presentations Career preparation The programme also lays the foundation for future employment through the World Bank Treasury Junior Analyst Programme. Internship Duration The internship lasts 10 weeks and will take place from: 1 June 2027 to 9 August 2027 Participants are expected to: Work full-time Attend in person Be based in Washington, DC Participate throughout the entire programme Official Internship Calendar The programme follows a structured schedule: Onboarding Week 1 June – 4 June 2027 Rotation One 7 June – 25 June 2027 Rotation Two 28 June – 16 July 2027 Rotation Three 19 July – 6 August 2027 Capstone Presentation 9 August 2027 Observed Holidays 18 June 2027 5 July 2027 Internship Structure One of the defining features of the World Bank Treasury Summer Internship is its rotational learning model. Following onboarding, interns rotate through three different Treasury teams , spending approximately three weeks with each. This allows participants to: Experience different areas of Treasury operations. Develop a broader understanding of international financial markets. Work with professionals across multiple business functions. Explore different career paths within Treasury. Throughout the internship, participants also engage in: Weekly discussions with the Programme Director. Technical learning sessions. Career-focused workshops. Meetings with Treasury officers, managers, and directors. Networking events with Treasury professionals. Opportunities to meet the World Bank Vice President and Treasurer. Each rotation concludes with a presentation to the host team, while the internship ends with a capstone presentation delivered to Treasury management. Treasury Departments and Teams Treasury expects to recruit up to 17 interns across its 17 teams , with each intern following a unique rotation schedule. The main Treasury departments include: Capital Markets Teams include: CMDDO (Debt Origination) CMDIS (Investor Relations Sustainable Finance) Asset Management Including: TREAM (Treasury Asset Management) Treasury Client Solutions Structuring Including: TREAB (Asset Liability and Balance Sheet Management) TREFP (Financial Products) Treasury Partnerships Advisory Including: TREAP (Partnerships Engagements) TREPK (Advisory Knowledge) Treasury Operations Including: TREFT (Financial Technology) The World Bank notes that hiring teams and organizational needs may change over time. Salary and Benefits The World Bank Treasury Summer Internship is a paid internship . Interns work a total of 400 hours under a short-term temporary contract. Compensation includes: US Citizens: US$27.70 per hour (gross) Non-US Citizens: US$22.70 per hour (net) Additional benefits include: A World Bank-issued laptop. Structured professional training. Mentorship throughout the programme. Networking with Treasury professionals. Exposure to senior leadership. Visa sponsorship for eligible international participants. Visa Sponsorship The World Bank sponsors the appropriate U.S. work visa for successful international applicants who require authorization to work in the United States. This enables qualified students from outside the United States to participate fully in the internship. Mentorship and Professional Development Professional development is an integral part of the internship. Each participant is assigned: A mentor A rotation coordinator Mentors provide guidance on: Daily work assignments Career planning Technical development Professional communication Workplace expectations Interns also gain access to a Treasury community of nearly 500 professionals , creating valuable networking and learning opportunities. Capstone Project The internship concludes with a capstone project presented to Treasury management. This final presentation enables interns to demonstrate: Analytical thinking Financial knowledge Communication skills Research ability Professional growth Participants also present their work at the conclusion of each departmental rotation. Pathway to the Treasury Junior Analyst Programme The internship serves as the primary pathway into the World Bank Treasury Junior Analyst Programme . Following successful completion of the internship, selected participants may receive the opportunity to apply for Junior Analyst positions. Applications for Junior Analyst roles open after the internship concludes. Junior Analyst positions: Begin after graduation. Are awarded through a competitive selection process. Offer a one-year appointment. May be renewed for one additional year, for a maximum of two years. Potential hiring teams include: Capital Markets Debt Origination Investor Relations Sustainable Finance Treasury Asset Management Asset Liability and Balance Sheet Management Financial Products Partnerships Engagements Advisory Knowledge Financial Technology Eligibility Requirements Applicants must satisfy all of the following requirements: Be enrolled in the second-to-last year of the equivalent of a four-year college degree programme. Demonstrate an interest in finance, business, economics, or a related discipline. Be expected to graduate between December 2027 and September 2028 . Be in good academic standing. Be available to work full-time for the entire internship. Not have another employment contract or internship during the programme. Applicants found to have misrepresented information may be disqualified or dismissed. Required Application Documents Applicants must submit: One-page PDF cover letter. One-page PDF résumé. Optional Document Applicants may also submit: A maximum half-page PDF personal statement. The optional statement allows applicants to explain: Their background. Personal experiences. Perspectives. How they would contribute to Treasury s diverse workplace. Although optional, it offers an opportunity to strengthen the application. Diversity and Inclusion The World Bank Treasury values diversity and inclusion and considers them central to its success. Treasury employees represent: More than 60 nationalities. Staff are located in offices including: Washington Paris Tokyo Singapore Chennai Sofia The World Bank is an equal opportunity employer and states that it does not discriminate based on: Gender Race Ethnicity Religion Disability Sexual orientation Gender identity The organization emphasizes that excellence and opportunity should be accessible regardless of background. Evaluation and Selection Process Applications submitted through the official online portal undergo a structured review process. The recruitment process includes: Review of complete applications by the recruitment committee. Remote assessment for selected candidates. Interviews with shortlisted applicants. Final selection. According to the World Bank, all applicants will be notified of the outcome by December 2026 . How to Apply Interested applicants must complete the official online application through the World Bank Group careers portal . Applicants should prepare: A one-page cover letter. A one-page résumé. An optional personal statement. The World Bank also encourages applicants to use its suggested résumé and cover letter templates before submission. Why You Should Apply The World Bank Treasury Summer Internship offers a rare opportunity to launch a career in global finance while contributing to international development. Participants will benefit from: Paid professional work experience. Competitive hourly compensation. Exposure to global financial markets. Rotations across multiple Treasury departments. Technical and leadership development. Mentorship from experienced finance professionals. Networking opportunities with senior Treasury leaders. A capstone presentation to Treasury management. Visa sponsorship for eligible international students. A pathway into the World Bank Treasury Junior Analyst Programme. For students interested in banking, investment management, treasury, capital markets, sustainable finance, economics, or development finance, this programme provides exceptional practical experience within one of the world s most respected international financial institutions. Click HERE to learn more about the World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027 Application Deadline Applications for the World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027 are currently open. The official announcement does not specify a closing deadline for applications. Interested students are encouraged to submit their applications as early as possible through the official World Bank careers portal. Conclusion The World Bank Treasury Summer Internship 2027 offers an exceptional opportunity for talented undergraduate students to gain hands-on experience in international finance while contributing to the mission of one of the world s leading development institutions. Through a structured 10-week programme in Washington, DC, interns rotate across Treasury departments, receive mentorship from experienced professionals, participate in technical and leadership development sessions, and complete meaningful projects that strengthen both their technical expertise and professional skills. Beyond offering competitive compensation and visa sponsorship for eligible international participants, the internship also provides a direct pathway into the World Bank Treasury Junior Analyst Programme, making it an ideal opportunity for students seeking long-term careers in finance, investment, treasury operations, and international development. Eligible applicants should prepare their application materials carefully and apply as early as possible to maximize their chances of joining this highly selective programme. Discover more global Internship opportunities and Summer programs on OFY. title: Community Accelerator Grant 2026: Up to $25,000 in Unrestricted Funding for Cultural Nonprofits Across Washington State
summary: The Community Accelerator Grant 2026 is a significant funding opportunity designed to strengthen Washington State s arts and cultural ecosystem by providing unrestricted financial support to eligible cultural nonprofit organizations. Administered by ArtsFund in partnership with Allen Family Philanthropies , the program represents the fourth year of an ambitious $10 million investment dedicated to helping arts organizations continue serving their communities while expanding their long-term impact. Unlike many traditional grant programs that restrict how funding can be spent, the Community Accelerator Grant provides flexible, unrestricted funding , allowing organizations to determine where resources are needed most. This flexibility enables nonprofits to strengthen operations, invest in programming, build organizational capacity, support staff, expand outreach efforts, and respond to evolving community needs. Following the tremendous success of previous funding rounds, the 2026 program continues its commitment to supporting organizations that play a vital role in preserving, promoting, and advancing arts and culture throughout Washington State. About the Community Accelerator Grant The Community Accelerator Grant was established to provide meaningful financial support to cultural nonprofit organizations that enrich communities through arts, heritage, culture, education, creativity, and public engagement. The initiative recognizes that arts and culture organizations are essential contributors to community well-being, economic vitality, cultural identity, education, and social cohesion. By offering unrestricted funding, ArtsFund allows recipient organizations to make independent decisions regarding the best use of grant resources based on their unique missions and priorities. The grant is fully funded through Allen Family Philanthropies , demonstrating a long-term commitment to strengthening Washington State s cultural infrastructure. A Proven Investment in Washington s Arts Sector The Community Accelerator Grant has already made a remarkable impact. During the 2025 funding cycle , the program: Awarded 930 grants Reached organizations across all 39 counties in Washington State Continued investing in nonprofit organizations serving diverse communities Strengthened local cultural institutions through unrestricted financial support This broad statewide reach illustrates the program s commitment to ensuring that arts organizations, regardless of location, have opportunities to receive meaningful support. Grant Amount Successful applicants may receive unrestricted grants ranging from: Minimum Award: $2,500 Maximum Award: $25,000 Because these grants are unrestricted, organizations have the flexibility to determine how the funding will best advance their mission and support the communities they serve. Why Unrestricted Funding Matters Many nonprofit grants come with detailed spending requirements that limit how organizations can use awarded funds. The Community Accelerator Grant takes a different approach. Organizations have the freedom to invest funding in areas such as: Organizational sustainability Operational expenses Community programs Staff support and development Capacity building Artistic programming Strategic planning Infrastructure improvements Administrative needs Community engagement initiatives Mission-driven priorities identified by organizational leadership This flexible funding model empowers nonprofits to address their most pressing needs rather than adapting their work to fit restrictive funding categories. Purpose of the Community Accelerator Grant The primary objective of the Community Accelerator Grant is to strengthen Washington State s arts and cultural sector by enabling organizations to make independent decisions about how to best serve their communities. The program seeks to: Strengthen arts and cultural organizations across Washington State. Promote a more inclusive and thriving cultural landscape. Support organizations serving diverse communities. Increase organizational resilience. Build long-term sustainability. Encourage community-centered cultural programming. Help nonprofits fulfill their missions more effectively. 2026 Eligibility Focus For the 2026 funding cycle, ArtsFund has introduced an important change to the eligibility framework. The fourth year of the Community Accelerator Grant will prioritize organizations with: Annual operating budgets of $1 million or less This adjustment recognizes that unrestricted funding can have an especially transformative impact on small and mid-sized nonprofit organizations. ArtsFund has also noted that: Additional eligibility requirements will be released during the summer. Organizations that qualified during previous funding cycles are not automatically eligible for the 2026 grant. Applicants should carefully review the updated eligibility guidelines before submitting an application. Application Timeline Applicants should pay close attention to the official application schedule. Application Opens Date: Wednesday, July 15, 2026 Time: 12:00 noon Application Deadline Date: Friday, August 21, 2026 Time: 5:00 p.m. Late applications are generally not accepted, so organizations are encouraged to prepare their materials well before the deadline. Grant Distribution Timeline Organizations selected for funding can expect grants to be distributed by: December 31, 2026 This timeline allows recipients to begin planning how they will utilize their unrestricted funding before the end of the calendar year. Community-Centered Decision Making One of the distinguishing features of the Community Accelerator Grant is its community-informed review process. Funding decisions are guided by a Community Advisory Panel that reflects: Geographic diversity Demographic diversity Community representation Local perspectives The panel helps ensure that grant decisions remain closely connected to the communities the organizations serve. This approach promotes equitable funding practices while recognizing the diverse needs of cultural organizations throughout Washington State. Accessibility and Inclusive Application Process ArtsFund is committed to ensuring that the application process is accessible to all eligible applicants. Accessibility measures include: Screen reader compatibility testing for the online application portal. Downloadable copy of the application. Accessibility assistance upon request. Direct support from the grantmaking team. Organizations with additional accessibility needs are encouraged to contact the grantmaking team before submitting their application. APPLY HERE Multilingual Resources Recognizing Washington State s diverse communities, ArtsFund has translated key application materials into 10 languages , making the program more accessible to a broad range of nonprofit organizations. Available languages include: Spanish (Español) French (Français) Japanese (日本語) Korean (한국어) Portuguese (Brazilian and European) Russian (Русский) Somali Tagalog Vietnamese (Tiếng Việt) Simplified Chinese (简体中文) These translated resources include: Application information Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) Demographic Data Use Statement Information Session Prospective applicants can participate in an online Zoom information session that provides detailed guidance about the application process. The webinar covers: Application requirements Updated eligibility criteria Program overview Question-and-answer session with ArtsFund staff Accessibility features available during the session include: American Sign Language (ASL) interpretation Automatic speech recognition (ASR) captions in more than 30 languages Visual descriptions These resources help ensure that applicants receive clear guidance before submitting their proposals. Frequently Asked Questions Applicants are encouraged to review the official Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) before applying. The FAQ section provides guidance on: Eligibility Application requirements Funding process Grant administration Program policies Technical questions Reviewing these materials can help applicants prepare stronger applications and better understand program expectations. Stay Updated ArtsFund encourages interested organizations to subscribe to its statewide newsletter to receive: Program announcements Eligibility updates Important deadlines Funding news Application guidance Future grant opportunities Since additional eligibility details will be released during the summer, subscribing to updates can help organizations stay informed. Contact Information Organizations with questions regarding the Community Accelerator Grant may contact the ArtsFund grantmaking team. Applicants should allow approximately three business days for responses to inquiries. title: UNICEF BeGreen Africa 3.0 Green Entrepreneurship Programme 2026: Apply for Green Business Training, Mentorship, Seed Funding of Up to USD 5,000, Incubation, and Market Support in South Africa and Nigeria
summary: Young entrepreneurs across South Africa and Nigeria now have an exciting opportunity to transform innovative green business ideas into sustainable enterprises through the UNICEF BeGreen Africa 3.0 Green Entrepreneurship Programme . Designed to empower youth with the skills, knowledge, financial support, and business networks needed to succeed in the green economy, this initiative seeks to strengthen environmentally sustainable entrepreneurship while creating meaningful employment opportunities. As climate change, unemployment, and economic inequality continue to affect millions of young people across Africa, UNICEF is investing in a new generation of entrepreneurs capable of developing businesses that not only generate income but also contribute to environmental sustainability. The programme combines entrepreneurship education, mentorship, technical skills development, incubation, seed funding, and market linkages into a comprehensive support system for aspiring and existing green entrepreneurs. Application Deadline: 29 July 2026. About the UNICEF BeGreen Africa 3.0 Programme The BeGreen Africa 3.0 Green Entrepreneurship Programme is an initiative developed by UNICEF to nurture young entrepreneurs interested in building businesses that solve environmental challenges while creating sustainable livelihoods. Rather than offering financial support alone, the programme provides participants with an entire entrepreneurship development pathway that equips them with practical skills, expert guidance, financial literacy, incubation support, and opportunities to connect with investors and markets. The programme is currently being implemented in: Gauteng, South Africa Lagos, Nigeria Through this initiative, UNICEF hopes to accelerate the growth of youth-led green enterprises while promoting climate-friendly innovation and inclusive economic development. Objectives of the Programme The BeGreen Africa 3.0 Programme has been designed to achieve several important objectives, including: Developing green entrepreneurship skills among young people. Supporting youth employment through sustainable businesses. Promoting environmentally responsible enterprises. Strengthening youth-owned Green Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs). Increasing women s participation in the green economy. Improving access to finance and investment opportunities. Connecting entrepreneurs to valuable business networks. Promoting sustainable economic growth across participating countries. Building on Previous Success BeGreen Africa 3.0 represents the continuation of earlier programme phases that successfully supported thousands of young entrepreneurs. Previous editions helped participants develop valuable expertise in: Green business models Sustainable enterprise development Waste management solutions Business management Entrepreneurship skills Many entrepreneurs who participated in earlier phases benefited from seed funding, expanded their businesses, created employment opportunities, and achieved significant business growth. The third phase builds on these achievements by providing an even stronger pathway from entrepreneurial learning to business establishment and expansion. Major Components of the Programme 1. Green Entrepreneurship Training Participants will receive comprehensive training covering: Sustainable business models Green innovation Environmental problem-solving Climate-smart entrepreneurship Opportunity identification within the green economy The training is intended to help young entrepreneurs transform environmental challenges into commercially viable business opportunities. 2. Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) The programme integrates Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) to equip participants with practical industry-relevant skills. Training areas include: Technical skills development Vocational learning Industry knowledge Green skills enhancement This practical learning ensures entrepreneurs possess both business knowledge and technical expertise. 3. Mentorship and Business Coaching One of the programme s strongest features is continuous mentorship from experienced professionals. Participants benefit from: One-on-one business coaching Entrepreneurial mentorship Expert technical advice Strategic business planning support Growth planning assistance This personalized guidance helps entrepreneurs overcome challenges and make informed business decisions. 4. Business Development Support Participants also receive support in developing and strengthening their businesses through: Business plan development Financial strategy formulation Market positioning Growth planning Enterprise expansion strategies The goal is to build businesses that remain sustainable beyond the programme period. 5. Financial Literacy and Access to Finance Financial management remains one of the most critical aspects of entrepreneurship. The programme therefore equips participants with skills in: Budget preparation Financial planning Investment readiness Financial management Understanding funding opportunities Outstanding entrepreneurs may also receive seed funding to support business growth. 6. Market Linkages and Business Networks Beyond training, participants gain access to valuable business ecosystems through: Industry partnerships Business networks Market opportunities Entrepreneurial communities These connections improve long-term sustainability and commercial success. Programme Structure The BeGreen Africa 3.0 Programme follows a structured multi-stage entrepreneurship development model. Stage 1: Foundational Training The programme aims to reach approximately: 20,000 young people Participants receive introductory learning covering: Green entrepreneurship Sustainable business opportunities Environmental challenges Climate solutions Stage 2: Advanced Learning Selected participants continue with: Design sprints Business planning Innovation exercises Entrepreneurship coaching Stage 3: Pitching Entrepreneurs present their ideas through: Business pitches Evaluations Expert feedback sessions Stage 4: Seed Funding and Incubation Up to: 45 young entrepreneurs will receive: Seed funding of up to USD 5,000 each Business incubation Growth support Continued mentorship Why This Programme Matters Africa continues to experience high youth unemployment alongside increasing climate-related challenges. The BeGreen Africa 3.0 Programme seeks to address both issues simultaneously by: Supporting environmentally sustainable businesses. Encouraging youth innovation. Creating green employment. Promoting women-led enterprises. Driving inclusive economic growth. Supporting climate-friendly entrepreneurship. By investing in youth-led green enterprises, UNICEF contributes to a more sustainable and resilient African economy. Expected Outcomes Successful implementation of the programme is expected to produce: Stronger Green Enterprises Participants will gain: Entrepreneurial skills Technical expertise Financial literacy Market connections Increased Employment Supported businesses are expected to contribute to: Job creation Local economic development Youth employment Greater Women s Economic Inclusion Young women entrepreneurs will receive increased access to: Training Mentorship Funding opportunities Business networks Evidence for Future Expansion The programme also includes: Continuous monitoring End-line assessments Research activities Learning reports These findings will inform future expansion across Africa. How the Programme Works Participants progress through the following stages: Youth mobilization through community outreach and entrepreneurship networks. Skills training focused on entrepreneurship, green business, and financial literacy. Business idea development, including business plans and growth strategies. Ongoing mentorship from experts and technical advisors. Seed funding and incubation support for selected businesses. Tips for Strong Applications Organizations supporting implementation should demonstrate: Experience working with youth. Expertise in green entrepreneurship. Capacity to provide training and mentorship. Business incubation experience. Monitoring and evaluation expertise. Strong submissions should clearly explain: Implementation plans Community engagement strategies Inclusion approaches Previous entrepreneurship experience APPLY HERE Common Mistakes to Avoid Applicants should avoid: Demonstrating limited experience in youth entrepreneurship. Weak expertise in green economy initiatives. Poor monitoring plans. Weak community engagement approaches. Ignoring gender inclusion priorities. Frequently Asked Questions Who can participate? The programme supports young entrepreneurs, youth-led green MSMEs, start-ups, and aspiring entrepreneurs interested in sustainable businesses. Where is the programme available? Implementation takes place in: Gauteng, South Africa Lagos, Nigeria How many participants will receive training? Approximately 20,000 young people will receive foundational green entrepreneurship training. How much funding is available? Selected entrepreneurs may receive seed funding of up to USD 5,000 . Are women encouraged to apply? Yes. Young women entrepreneurs are among the programme s priority beneficiaries. What areas does the programme cover? The initiative focuses on green entrepreneurship, business development, financial literacy, mentorship, incubation, market access, sustainable enterprise development, and youth employment. Visit HERE to learn more about the UNICEF BeGreen Africa 3.0 Green Entrepreneurship Programme 2026 Conclusion The UNICEF BeGreen Africa 3.0 Green Entrepreneurship Programme offers a comprehensive opportunity for young entrepreneurs to build environmentally sustainable businesses while gaining practical entrepreneurial skills, expert mentorship, financial literacy, business incubation, market access, and potential seed funding. By prioritizing youth innovation, women s economic inclusion, and climate-friendly enterprise development, the programme is helping shape a greener and more inclusive future for South Africa and Nigeria. Aspiring entrepreneurs with innovative green business ideas are encouraged to prepare strong applications before the deadline of 29 July 2026 to take advantage of this transformative initiative. title: ABC Initiative Implementation Grants 2026: Up to $150,000 in Funding for Nonprofit VITA Organizations Supporting Child Savings Access
summary: Access to savings opportunities from an early age can play a transformative role in helping children build long-term financial security. Recognizing this, Prosperity Now , with support from the Citi Foundation , has launched the ABC Initiative (Asset Building for Children) and is now accepting applications for its first round of Implementation Grants . Through this initiative, eligible nonprofit organizations operating IRS-sponsored Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) programs can receive grants ranging from $25,000 to $150,000 to help families access child savings opportunities during tax season. Backed by $5 million in funding from Citi Foundation , the ABC Initiative seeks to strengthen financial inclusion by integrating child savings education, referrals, and support into tax-time services. The initiative focuses on helping families understand emerging child savings opportunities, including Section 530A Accounts , while equipping nonprofit organizations with the tools, training, and financial resources needed to expand their impact. If your nonprofit organization operates a VITA program and serves low- and moderate-income families, this grant opportunity provides significant funding and technical support to strengthen your services while contributing to a national effort to improve children s financial futures. About the ABC Initiative The Asset Building for Children (ABC) Initiative is a national program led by Prosperity Now to help more families access child savings opportunities across the United States. The initiative was created to ensure that families receive clear information, practical guidance, and personalized support in understanding and accessing child savings accounts. Through this effort, Prosperity Now aims to increase participation in existing and emerging child savings programs while reducing barriers that prevent many families from taking advantage of these opportunities. Supported by a $5 million investment from the Citi Foundation , the first phase of the initiative focuses on helping nonprofit organizations integrate child savings guidance into IRS-sponsored Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) services. Why the ABC Initiative Matters Tax season is often one of the most important financial periods for many families. During this time, households make key decisions regarding: Tax refunds Tax credits Household budgets Savings Future financial planning Because VITA programs already provide trusted tax preparation services to millions of families, Prosperity Now believes these organizations are ideally positioned to educate parents about child savings opportunities while helping them make informed financial decisions. The initiative seeks to ensure that more children begin adulthood with stronger financial foundations by connecting families with savings opportunities during one of their most financially significant moments of the year. Grant Funding Available Eligible organizations selected for the first implementation cohort will receive grants ranging from: Minimum Award: $25,000 Maximum Award: $150,000 Final grant amounts will depend on several factors, including: Anticipated number of families served. Scope of the proposed implementation. Organizational capacity. Proposed project activities. Budget quality. Overall funding availability. What the Grant Will Support Funding can be used to strengthen tax-time services by incorporating child savings education and support into existing VITA operations. Examples of eligible activities include: Integrating child savings information into VITA intake and tax preparation workflows. Providing child savings education during tax preparation appointments. Offering referrals and navigation support for child savings opportunities. Conducting community outreach before and during tax season. Training staff and volunteers on child savings programs. Developing partnerships with financial institutions and community organizations. Hosting tax-season outreach and activation events. Improving internal systems and workflows. Collecting implementation data and participating in evaluation activities. Supporting continuous improvement of service delivery. Additional Support Beyond Grant Funding Successful applicants will receive much more than financial support. Awardees will benefit from: Comprehensive onboarding. Professional training. Ongoing technical assistance. Implementation guidance. Digital financial coaching resources where applicable. Practical implementation tools. Peer learning opportunities. Participation in a national learning community. Evaluation support. Standardized reporting guidance. Opportunities to contribute to national best practices. Who Can Apply? Applications are invited from eligible nonprofit organizations that operate IRS-sponsored Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) programs. To qualify, organizations must: Be a nonprofit organization. Operate an IRS-sponsored VITA program. Serve as the lead applicant and fiscal agent. Be responsible for grant management and reporting. Demonstrate readiness to integrate child savings support into tax-time services. Serve low- and moderate-income households. Have strong community relationships. Participate fully in training, evaluation, and peer learning. Community organizations, schools, financial institutions, and public agencies may participate as partners, but the lead applicant must be an eligible VITA provider. Geographic Eligibility Priority will be given to organizations serving the following locations: California Alameda Contra Costa Fresno Kern Kings Los Angeles Marin Merced Orange Riverside Sacramento San Bernardino San Diego San Francisco San Joaquin San Mateo Santa Clara Stanislaus Ventura Connecticut Greater Bridgeport Planning Region Western Connecticut Planning Region Florida Broward Miami-Dade Palm Beach Illinois Cook DuPage Kane Lake McHenry Will Maryland Montgomery Prince George s Nevada Clark County New Jersey Bergen Essex Hudson Passaic New York Bronx Kings Nassau New York Queens Richmond Rockland Suffolk Westchester Puerto Rico Territory-wide South Dakota State-wide Virginia Alexandria City Arlington Fairfax Fairfax City Falls Church City Washington, D.C. Entire District of Columbia APPLY HERE Preferred Applicant Experience Organizations are encouraged to have experience in areas such as: Children s Savings Accounts Section 530A Accounts Baby Bonds 529 Savings Plans Financial coaching Tax-time savings initiatives Family financial capability However, prior child savings experience is not required . Organizations operating only Tax Counseling for the Elderly (TCE) programs are not eligible . Selection Criteria Applications will be evaluated based on: Quality of the implementation approach. Organizational capacity and readiness. Family and community reach. Strength of partnerships. Evaluation and learning capacity. Budget quality and feasibility. Applicants should clearly demonstrate how they will integrate child savings education into their existing VITA services. Participation Requirements Successful organizations will be expected to: Implement the approved project. Attend onboarding sessions. Participate in required training. Join technical assistance activities. Engage in peer learning. Train staff and volunteers. Build strategic partnerships. Collect and report project data. Participate in programme evaluation. Share lessons learned with the national cohort. Reporting Requirements Awardees will submit both quantitative and qualitative reports covering: Staff and volunteer training completion. Families reached. Child savings interactions. Referrals made. Account enrollments where applicable. Partnerships established. Organizational capacity improvements. Lessons learned. Implementation challenges. Best practices. Reporting guidance and standardized tools will be provided during onboarding. Important Dates Applicants should carefully note the following timeline: Activity Date Request for Proposals Released July 20, 2026 Application Deadline August 7, 2026 Conditional Award Notifications September 2, 2026 Final Award Agreements Public Announcement September 16, 2026 ABC Initiative Cohort Launch VITACon October 6, 2026 Training Preparation Fall 2026 2027 Tax Season Implementation January–April 2027 How to Apply Interested organizations should: Review all eligibility requirements. Download and study the 2026 Request for Proposals (RFP). Review the Application Planning Guide. Gather all required documentation. Submit their application through Prosperity Now s online application portal no later than August 7, 2026 . Applicants with questions may contact Prosperity Now through the email address provided in the official call for proposals. Why Organizations Should Apply Participating organizations will benefit from: Grant funding of up to $150,000. National recognition through participation in the ABC Initiative. Capacity-building support. Staff and volunteer training. Technical assistance. Access to implementation resources. Peer networking opportunities. Evaluation support. Opportunities to strengthen services for low-income families. The ability to contribute to improving financial security for children across the United States. Visit HERE to learn more about the ABC Initiative Implementation Grants 2026 Deadline Application Deadline: August 7, 2026 Late applications are unlikely to be considered. Eligible organizations are encouraged to begin preparing their applications as early as possible. Conclusion The ABC Initiative Implementation Grants 2026 represent an outstanding funding opportunity for nonprofit organizations committed to expanding financial opportunity for children and families. Through generous grants of up to $150,000 , extensive technical assistance, professional training, and participation in a national learning community, Prosperity Now is equipping VITA providers to become trusted pathways for child savings education and access. Organizations serving low- and moderate-income families have a unique opportunity to strengthen their tax-time services while helping families build long-term financial security for future generations. If your organization operates an eligible IRS-sponsored VITA program and serves one of the target communities, this initiative offers both the financial resources and strategic support needed to make a lasting impact.
